title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Public Works Committee approved several items including acknowledging the 2025 Water Utility PSC annual report, adopting a resolution supporting state transportation funding, awarding a $126,900 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel, and directing staff to create enforcement language for sewer discharge violations with fines starting at $100 per day. The committee also approved construction agreements for Military Road and Radkey Avenue utilities, and selected consultants for upcoming road projects.

agenda.item: Call to Order and Pledge of Allegiance
Roll Call
Public Comment
Approval of April 13, 2026 Meeting Minutes
Acknowledge April 2026 Water and Sewer Permits
CIP Update
Review 2025 Water Utility PSC Annual Reports
Resolution 2026-014 State Transportation Fund
Recycled Asphalt Base Course Production
Sanitary Sewer Discharge Violation Discussion
Water and Sewer Connection Agreement - Radkey Avenue
Military Road Construction Agreement

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement Grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Schofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, originally planned for 2029 but likely moving to 2028. The grant covers about 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project cost. Michael noted the village has received over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion also covered Weston Avenue settlement repairs by Integrity Grading and Excavating, Schofield Avenue repairs with HOS Construction, and Fuller Street restoration with Melvin. A special assessment meeting for Bladel Avenue was tentatively scheduled for Thursday, May 28 at 6:00 PM.

Staff reported road limits were lifted April 29 along with Marathon County. Spring activities included mailbox repairs, sign repairs, curb line sweeping, and hot mix patching. Storm damage cleanup occurred off East Nick. Dewatering pumps ran for about a week near Weston Avenue tributary to manage flooding near Crane Meadows. Discussion ensued about potential permanent pump solutions for recurring flooding issues in the Zenzer/Shorey Avenue area. Salt supply was addressed - an additional 1,500 tons ordered for November delivery with current inventory around 1,200 tons.

Josh reported on water service repairs at Caribou Coffee, well maintenance, and ongoing private well sampling. Significant clear water infiltration was identified in the sanitary sewer system - April showed 37 million gallons of water pumped into the system but 53-54 million gallons treated, indicating approximately 15 million gallons of clear water infiltration. The Kathleen lift station was specifically identified as problematic with suspected sump pump discharges. An INI (infiltration/inflow) study with in-pipe technologies is planned to identify problem areas. Water main flushing notices were being mailed with flushing to begin the following week.

Michael presented the annual report showing operating revenues and expenses resulting in net income of approximately $482,911 for 2025, consistent with 2024's $490,000. Water loss was reported at 6%. The utility's value increased to nearly $34 million with $6.7 million in infrastructure placed in service including Wells 7 and 8 and water main from the Weston Avenue project. The utility serves approximately 6,100 customers total - 5,250 in Weston, 830 in Rothschild, and 5 in other areas. PAS settlement funds of $1.44 million were received. The report was acknowledged unanimously.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities sample resolution supports improved state transportation funding for day-to-day operations rather than just grant-funded one-time projects. He noted the village has decreased its public works budget the last couple years and highlighted Wisconsin's relatively affordable automobile ownership costs compared to neighboring states. The committee unanimously recommended the village board adopt the resolution.

Michael presented quotes for crushing recycled material at the Ryan Street site. King Gravel submitted the low bid at $4.23 per ton for an estimated 30,000 tons totaling $126,900. Comparing to Wausau's costs of $28 per cubic yard versus Weston's $17.11 per cubic yard, the village saves approximately $130,000 across three projects this year. The contract was unanimously awarded to King Gravel.

Staff sought guidance on enforcement fines for illegal clear water discharges into the sanitary sewer system. Current code allows fines between $100-$1,000 per day. Discussion centered on whether violations were unknowing versus willful. Trustee Jordan suggested $100 per day is adequate as it accumulates quickly. Committee directed staff to wait for the INI study results to identify problem areas, then send notices giving residents 30-60 days to fix issues before implementing $100 per day fines. Motion passed unanimously.

Michael explained a duplex developer at 2230 Radkey Avenue needs utility connections, but extending mains through Outlot 1 immediately would require the developer to install them. Staff proposed allowing temporary long lateral connections with the agreement that the owner will pay assessments when Radkey Avenue is reconstructed. Committee requested adding a specific date - no later than 2029 - to the agreement. Motion passed unanimously with the date modification.

Michael presented an intergovernmental agreement with Rothschild for Military Road reconstruction from Wolfman Street to Helin Street. Steam Construction is the contractor. Weston's share for water main relocation and sanitary sewer work is estimated at $94,860. The water main must be relocated due to conflicts with storm sewer improvements. The agreement was unanimously recommended for board approval.

Michael presented consultant proposals for construction inspection services since the engineering tech position remains vacant. For the Alderson/Jelinek roundabout project, MSA proposed $37,315 versus Clark Dietz at $48,910 - MSA recommended. For Bladel Avenue, MSA proposed $70,400 and Clark Dietz $73,730 - Clark Dietz recommended due to having full-time experienced staff rather than a summer intern. Both recommendations passed unanimously.

actionItems: Award 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel at $4.23/ton (estimated $126,900)
Recommend village board adopt Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding
Direct staff to create enforcement language for sewer discharge violations at $100/day fine
Approve Radkey Avenue utility connection agreement with assessment due no later than 2029
Recommend village board approve Military Road construction agreement with Rothschild for $94,860
Select MSA for Alderson/Jelinek roundabout construction services ($37,315)
Select Clark Dietz for Bladel Avenue construction services ($73,730)
Schedule Bladel Avenue special assessment meeting for May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM
Staff to provide cost analysis of clear water infiltration treatment for next meeting
Norcon to begin Schofield Avenue concrete repairs starting Wednesday/Thursday

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved the site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and adopted a salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection areas. Both items passed unanimously with staff conditions.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Open public comment period
Approval of April 13, 2026 meeting minutes
Reordering of agenda items
Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Creation of salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
April 2026 staff approved certified survey map and site plan
April 2026 building permit report
Planning and development department monthly project update
Commissioner remarks
Future agenda items and adjournment

discussions.item: Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility site plan
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive. Jen from staff explained the project meets all B2 zoning requirements and landscaping code. Chief Jeremy explained the building was rotated to face east due to a 36-inch gas pipeline running under the parking lot, which significantly reduced fill costs. The building will accommodate 50-60 officers compared to current staffing of 40, with expansion capability to the north. Discussion included the alternate storage shed placement to allow for future building expansion. Motion to approve with staff conditions 2-5 passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
discussions.body: Staff proposed a two-inspection-per-year program for salt storage facilities in wellhead protection areas - one warm weather inspection focusing on structural integrity and one winter operational inspection. The program includes a standardized inspection worksheet similar to DOT standards and a classification system for deficiencies. Staff noted one existing facility near the village well on Sternberg has never gone through the wellhead protection conditional use process and will be contacted. Joel Garant commended the program. Motion to approve with reporting to both Plan Commission and Public Works Committee passed unanimously.

discussions.item: April 2026 staff approved certified survey map and site plan
discussions.body: Motion to approve passed unanimously with no discussion.
Motion to approve passed unanimously with no discussion.

Staff announced the first meeting of the joint Plan Commission-Village Board steering committee for the comprehensive plan update is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. Staff will send calendar invitations. Discussion touched on potential residential TIF districts for workforce housing, with Ehlers having prepared a presentation that may be shared with the commission or incorporated into comprehensive plan discussions.

publicComment: Jim Pensel of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via online participation. He urged the commission to exercise common sense on landscaping requirements for the police department project to avoid excessive taxpayer costs, referencing relaxed standards applied to the Green Heck Turner building. He also requested the commission require annual reporting on the salt shed inspection program to ensure oversight doesn't get 'lost in the sauce' like some other zoning complaints.
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive approved with staff conditions 2-5
Salt storage shed inspection program adopted for wellhead protection district with two annual inspections per facility
Staff to report inspection program findings to Plan Commission and Public Works Committee in approximately one year
Staff to contact existing salt storage facility on Sternberg about wellhead protection conditional use compliance
Joint Plan Commission-Village Board steering committee meeting scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Staff to send calendar invitations for steering committee meeting
Electric vehicle charging station regulations to be addressed in fall agenda

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an educational orientation meeting for new board members, featuring presentations from department heads on the Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Service Office, and Aging Disability Resource Center. No policy votes were taken; the meeting focused on introducing committee structure, department missions, and budgets to new supervisors.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call (remote participants noted)
Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operational functions required by statute - overview of agenda structure
Review of standing committee ordinance section 2.04, including missions, duties, and responsibilities
Health Department presentation by Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka
Social Services Department overview by Administrator Lance Leonard
Veteran Service Office presentation by Veteran Service Officer Erin Galindo
Aging Disability Resource Center presentation by Executive Director Mike Ray
Next meeting date and adjournment

discussions.item: Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
discussions.body: Supervisor Cavalli moved to approve the minutes, Vice Chair Rosenberg seconded. The motion passed unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Review of standing committee ordinance and structure
discussions.body: Administrator Lance Leonard explained the committee's jurisdiction, including oversight of the Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Services, North Central Health Care, and the Aging Disability Resource Center. He outlined statutory responsibilities, reporting relationships, and affiliated boards including the Social Services Board, Veteran Service Commission, Transportation Coordinating Committee, and Board of Health. Chair Boots noted the committee will focus on the strategic plan development over the next year.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented the department's mission of preventing disease, promoting health, and protecting against environmental hazards. She highlighted over 1,200 licenses overseen, communicable disease monitoring, the Nurse Family Partnership program with its first graduates, public health vending machines stocked with free resources including tick kits, and a new homeless system response position. The department has approximately 37.6 FTE staff organized into environmental health, nursing, community health improvement, and administrative teams.

Administrator Leonard provided an overview of the department's 140 FTE staff covering child welfare, youth justice, children's long-term support, economic support, and child support programs. He highlighted the successful Family Keys housing stability pilot program developed with board input, noting housing instability was a growing concern. He mentioned potential restructuring requests may come during budget season to address increased service needs.

Veteran Service Officer Erin Galindo reported the office increased from 2.75 to 3.0 FTE in 2025. Customer interactions jumped from approximately 5,900 to 8,149 in 2025. The PACT Act has significantly increased claims. Annual VA compensation flowing to Marathon County veterans totals $44 million, with $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025. Key initiatives include surviving spouse benefits outreach, veteran housing stability partnerships with Center for Veterans Issues, and suicide prevention efforts (7 of 30 county suicides in 2025 were veterans). When asked by Supervisor Kremes about adequate staffing, Galindo acknowledged the office is very busy with staff averaging 20-30 daily contacts but stated they manage well with current resources.

Executive Director Mike Ray explained the ADRC serves four counties (Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, Wood) as a one-stop shop for aging and disability services. Programs include information assistance, caregiver support, dementia care, falls prevention, Medicare counseling, and Meals on Wheels nutrition services. He noted significant financial challenges with nutrition program costs rising 40-45% over four years while federal funding remains flat. The organization relies on approximately 385-400 volunteers. Vice Chair Rosenberg praised the culturally appropriate meal program at the Hmong American Center.

actionItems: Schedule North Central Healthcare for a separate full presentation before the committee
Chair Boots to present strategic plan information at upcoming meetings
Committee members to email agenda item suggestions to Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard
Next meeting confirmed for Tuesday, June 3rd at 3:00 PM (corrected from initially stated May 29th)

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board of Supervisors held an adjourned organizational meeting where they approved committee appointments, elected Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to the at-large Executive Committee positions, and unanimously approved a resolution to reduce landfill tipping fees for disaster debris following the recent tornado and flooding that devastated the town of Ringle. The board also heard from affected residents requesting additional financial relief.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and silent moment of reflection
Reading of the notice and roll call
Committee appointments discussion and remarks from Chair
Election of at-large Executive Committee members
Consent agenda items C10-C14A
Resolution D15 - Out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution D16 - Social services budget amendment
Resolution E17 - Landfill tipping fees modification
Public comment on landfill tipping fees
Announcements and adjournment

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs delivered remarks thanking staff and Vice Chair Boots for developing committee appointments, emphasizing goals of increasing diversity of perspective, developing leadership among the 34% of supervisors in their first or second term, and honoring supervisor preferences. The appointments were approved in a single motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Election of At-Large Executive Committee Members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated for two at-large positions: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Vice Chair Boots). Supervisor Rosenberg spoke in support of Supervisor Leur, citing her advocacy for the county's 'smallest children and oldest elders.' Paper ballots were distributed and counted. Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the Executive Committee.

discussions.item: Resolution 24-26 - Out of Home Placement Reserve Increase
discussions.body: Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. No discussion was held. The motion carried but was not unanimous.
Motion by Supervisor P, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. No discussion was held. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

Administrator Leonard and Solid Waste Director Hagen Busher presented the proposal following severe weather that devastated Ringle and other communities. The state declared an emergency, waiving the $13.10 state fee; the town of Ringle waived its $2.33 fee. The solid waste management board proposed reducing the county fee to $36.94 per ton (from $65 standard rate), essentially covering costs. Director Hagen Busher noted Brown County and Dane County did not offer similar relief. The board suspended rules (unanimous) to consider the resolution from the solid waste management board. Motion by Supervisor Arstead, second by Supervisor Morash. The resolution passed unanimously.

publicComment: Two residents were granted special dispensation to speak on the landfill tipping fee resolution. Jessica Toasty requested Marathon County wave all fees for six months, noting the state, DNR, and Ringle waived their fees, and that private businesses donated $40,000+ in cleanup assistance while residents face uninsured costs. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of residents with demolished homes, requesting fee waivers for underinsured homeowners and noting some insurance adjusters haven't arrived yet. He requested extending the waiver period beyond 60 days.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved as presented by Chair Gibbs and Vice Chair Boots
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to at-large Executive Committee positions
Resolution 24-26 approved for out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution 25-26 approved for social services budget amendment
Resolution 26-26 approved reducing landfill tipping fees to $36.94 per ton for certified disaster debris through June 17
Administrator Leonard to continue discussions with Community Foundation regarding reimbursement mechanism for unreimbursed insurance costs
Solid Waste Management Board to develop long-term policy on handling disaster-related debris within six months
Supervisors Rosenberg and Gao to be notified of future Solid Waste Board meetings

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions unanimously and passed an amended resolution to waive only reconnect fees for septic systems for properties impacted by the April 17, 2026 tornado. The committee also received educational presentations from Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning departments.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public comment
Approval of March 31st, 2026 committee minutes
Public hearing: Tim Veland on behalf of James Offer and David Trait rezoning petition
Public hearing: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition
Consideration of fee waivers for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Introduction to departments appearing before the committee
Solid Waste Department overview presentation
Parks, Recreation, and Forestry Department overview presentation
Conservation Planning and Zoning Department overview presentation

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition (Urban Residential and General Agriculture to Rural Residential)
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for a rezoning in the Town of Green Valley. The Town Board recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public comment was offered in favor, against, or as interested. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact, conclusions of law, and the town's approval, seconded by Supervisor Kurt. The motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture)
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning in the Town of McMillan. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy nature of the property; Pogle explained the property contains a home and horse riding stable, and the rezoning makes all structures conforming uses. The Town Board recommended approval on April 20, 2026. No public comment was offered. Supervisor Sefelt moved to approve, seconded by Supervisor Drew. The motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waivers for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
discussions.body: Director Lori Mskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and septic fees for tornado-impacted properties. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future incidents. Supervisor Leur asked about potential levy impacts. Supervisor Drebeck raised concerns about fairness compared to non-disaster victims and proposed an amendment to limit the waiver to only septic reconnect fees ($160). The amendment was seconded by Supervisor Kurt and passed. The amended resolution then passed with the committee voting to forward it to the county board, though the vote was not unanimous.

Director David Hagenbusher presented on department operations including the landfill, renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy, leachate treatment challenges, and upcoming capital projects. Marilyn Bend praised the department's work helping Ringle tornado victims. Supervisor Brook asked about the environmental loan application; Hagenbusher said the 2026 application was deemed incomplete and they are reapplying for 2027 funding.

discussions.item: Parks, Recreation, and Forestry Department overview
discussions.body: Tom Lovelin presented on behalf of Director Jamie, covering the department's dual role serving both Marathon County and City of Wausau, the 17 county parks, 30,700-acre county forest system, and Nine Mile County Forest's $3.25 million economic impact. Supervisor Sefelt noted this was Lovelin's last committee meeting before his June 1st retirement after 35 years, and thanked him for his service.

actionItems: Rezoning from Urban Residential and General Agriculture to Rural Residential for James Offer and David Trait property forwarded to County Board with approval recommendation
Rezoning from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture for Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz property forwarded to County Board with approval recommendation
Amended resolution to waive only septic reconnect fees (not other zoning or pouts fees) for tornado-impacted properties forwarded to County Board
CPZ to track costs and requests related to tornado fee waivers for future policy development
Solid Waste to resubmit environmental loan application for leachate treatment facility by October 2025 for 2027 funding

title: Tourism Commission
overview: Based on the published agenda, this special meeting of the Village of Weston Tourism Commission was scheduled to elect new officers and consider a $10,000 grant request for the Taste N Glow Balloon Fest. The meeting was set to address leadership positions for the commission and evaluate funding for a major annual event expected to draw 15,000 attendees to the area.

agenda.item: Election of Officers: Chair, Vice Chair, Secretary
Approval of minutes from the previous meeting: April 21, 2026
Grant Request: Taste N Glow Fest

discussions.body: The commission was scheduled to elect leadership positions including Chair, Vice Chair, and Secretary. Current commission members eligible for these positions include Ermeling, Frederick, Hodell, Jalowitz, and Zeyghami.

discussions.item: Grant Request: Taste N Glow Fest
discussions.body: The commission was set to consider a $10,000 room tax grant request from Taste N Glow Balloon Fest for their annual two-day event scheduled for July 10-11, 2026 in Marathon City. The event expects approximately 15,000 attendees and 45 estimated room nights, with funds to be used for advertising, potential shuttles, and lodging expenses for balloon pilots and crew members.

publicComment: Public comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Scheduled to vote on election of Chair, Vice Chair, and Secretary
Expected to consider approval of April 21, 2026 meeting minutes
Scheduled to vote on $10,000 grant request for Taste N Glow Balloon Fest

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Village of Weston Finance & Human Resource Committee was scheduled to meet on May 18, 2026 to review April financial reports including budget status and TIF district details, as well as receive a presentation on Clerk's Department operations and budget. This routine monthly meeting was set to provide fiscal oversight of village finances and departmental operations.

agenda.item: Approval of Minutes from Previous Finance & HR Meeting: April 21, 2026
April Budget Status Report
TIF 1 and TIF 2 Detail Reports for April
Legal Details for April
Discussion on Clerk's Department Operations and Budget

discussions.body: The committee was scheduled to acknowledge the April 2026 financial report for all funds. According to the attached report, general fund revenue stood at 70.3% of budget while expenditures were at 40.7% of budget, with staff monitoring several line items that may exceed their budgets.

discussions.item: TIF 1 and TIF 2 Detail Reports for April
discussions.body: The committee was set to review the monthly Tax Increment Financing district reports for TIF 1 and TIF 2. These reports provide ongoing monitoring of the village's tax increment financing districts.
The committee was expected to acknowledge the legal details report for April, providing oversight of the village's legal expenditures.

discussions.item: Discussion on Clerk's Department Operations and Budget
discussions.body: The committee was scheduled to receive an educational presentation on Clerk's Department operations and budget. This follows a similar presentation format from the previous month when Planning & Development operations were reviewed.

publicComment: Public comments section was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Scheduled to vote on approval of April 21, 2026 meeting minutes
Expected to acknowledge April Budget Status Report
Expected to acknowledge TIF 1 and TIF 2 Detail Reports for April
Expected to acknowledge Legal Details for April

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee approved several ordinance amendments and addressed multiple community issues including ebike regulations, overnight parking restrictions, mobile food vendor fire inspections, and a demolition order. Key actions included recommending approval of nuisance ordinance amendments, an ebike ordinance with restrictions on emotos, and authorizing demolition of an abandoned structure on Shore Avenue.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Roll call
Public comments
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Department reports - Code enforcement, Fire/EMS, Police
Proposed amendments to Chapter 50 nuisances
Ebike ordinance in Chapter 82 traffic and vehicles
Section 82.107 overnight parking regulations
Crosswalk request on Alderson Street
Mobile food vendors fire inspection amendments
Address corrections for E911 compliance
Raze request for 4104 Shore Avenue

discussions.body: Roman/Audrey reported that 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the code enforcement list after the property sale was completed and the new owner submitted permits for repairs. Chief Hunt reported 2,394 police calls in March (77 per day), 410 traffic stops, and 281 citations issued. The department has hired its 40th officer, with the 38th starting last week, 39th starting in May, and 40th starting around January. Two SROs are assigned to zoning violations during summer. Motion to acknowledge reports 5, 6, and 7 made by Shawanka, seconded by Olsen, passed unanimously.

Committee reviewed final version of nuisance ordinance amendments, including previously discussed changes to tree limb height requirements (14 and 10 feet switched) with compliance requirements for jurisdictions with authority. Motion to recommend approval to board of trustees made by Olsen, seconded by Kimi, passed unanimously.

Committee discussed proposed ebike regulations. Chief Hunt strongly recommended prohibiting emotos (electric dirt bike-style vehicles) from roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property due to safety concerns, noting they are essentially unlicensed dirt bikes. Discussion addressed whether electric scooters should be included in restrictions. Committee decided to allow officer discretion for children's play scooters. Motion by Olsen to recommend approval with emotos prohibited from roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property, seconded by Shawanka, passed unanimously. Rothschild has not yet approved; Schofield indicated they don't feel it's necessary.

Brent Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour parking restriction window (4am-4pm) is significantly longer than other municipalities (most have 5-hour windows). Michael from public works explained the daytime restriction was implemented because plowing typically doesn't start until 4am and residential streets aren't reached until 7-8am. Chief Hunt expressed preference for overnight restrictions to give officers time to clear vehicles before plowing. Committee discussed reducing hours and identifying specific problem areas. Decision was made to table the item until July, with Michael tasked to identify approximately 6-8 specific problem areas for targeted signage.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk between their property and DC Everest school where parishioners park. Michael estimated cost at approximately $2,000 for basic crosswalk, $25,000-30,000 for lighted system. Committee expressed reluctance to set precedent of paying for requested crosswalks. Chief Hunt noted no incidents in the area. Motion by Olsen that if Mount Olive agrees to cover installation costs, the committee recommends board approval, seconded by Shawanka, passed unanimously. Michael will contact Mount Olive.

Chief Hunt explained a new regional fire inspection program involving Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and Weston. One inspection with a sticker is valid for all participating municipalities. Wausau opted out to do their own inspections. Fire Marshal Marty already inspected one truck that failed due to improper LP piping (galvanized steel) and unsecured propane cylinders. Inspections are free. Motion by Shawanka to approve Ordinance 26-8, seconded by Campkey, passed unanimously.

Jen presented three addressing corrections: 5915 Delicoski Street (duplex needing separate addresses, owners agreed); Ross Avenue properties out of sequence near existing apartment building; and 2211 Radkey Avenue for new duplex on private drive. Chief Hunt identified an additional issue at Weston Villa Apartments on Delicoski with nine separate addresses for one structure. Motion made and seconded, passed unanimously.

Structure has been abandoned since 2010-2011 and posted since 2021. Previous fire department training burn was cancelled because structure was too deteriorated for safe exercise. Property owners are current on taxes and have preferred the raze order route. Owners had previously housed chickens inside the structure. Demolition costs will be placed on the tax roll. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senault spoke regarding multiple agenda items. He criticized the new farmers market location at the park, predicting grass destruction by end of summer from vehicle parking. He questioned whether current village code prohibiting parking on grass would apply. He noted ADA handicap spaces are currently on grass rather than adjacent asphalt, which he argued doesn't meet ADA requirements for firm, level surfaces. He questioned the origin of new fire inspections for food trucks. He also expressed concern about accountability for long-standing code violations, stating some cases have been on the list for years with no visible progress.
actionItems: Recommend Chapter 50 nuisance amendments to board of trustees for approval
Recommend ebike ordinance to board of trustees with emoto prohibition added for roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property
Michael to identify 6-8 specific problem areas for overnight parking restrictions and bring back to July meeting
Michael to contact Mount Olive Church regarding willingness to pay for crosswalk installation on Alderson Street
Recommend Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections to board
Recommend address corrections for 5915 Delicoski, Ross Avenue properties, and 2211 Radkey Avenue to board
Authorize staff to begin raze order process for 4104 Shore Avenue
Staff to investigate property at Sternberg and South Timber regarding furniture and dumpster in yard
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, July 6th at 5:00 p.m.

title: Parks Committee

The meeting included several community recreation facility matters relevant to Weston residents.

agenda.item: Approval of minutes from the previous meeting: April 27, 2026
Public Comments
Written Comments
Kennedy Update
Yellowbank Disc Golf Course Update

Discussion and possible action of Graffiti on the Eau Claire River Trail

discussions.body: Director Osterbrink was scheduled to provide an update on Kennedy Park developments. Based on previous meeting minutes, this was expected to include information on the capital campaign, grants, and ongoing renovation work.
The committee was set to receive an update on the Yellowbank Disc Golf Course. This was expected to cover current status and any developments related to the facility.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the Aquatic Center
discussions.body: The committee was scheduled to discuss and potentially take action on holding a Dog Day event at the Aquatic Center. This type of event typically allows dogs to swim at the pool at the end of the summer season.

discussions.item: Graffiti on the Eau Claire River Trail
discussions.body: The committee was expected to consider how to address graffiti issues on the Eau Claire River Trail. Discussion was scheduled to potentially include response measures or prevention strategies.
The committee was set to discuss and potentially approve signage for the Yellowbanks Kayak Launch. This follows previous discussions about the launch facility and its amenities.

publicComment: Public Comments and Written Comments were both listed as agenda items.
actionItems: Scheduled to vote on approval of April 27, 2026 meeting minutes
Expected to consider action on Dog Day at the Aquatic Center
Expected to consider action regarding graffiti on the Eau Claire River Trail
Expected to consider action on Yellowbanks Kayak Launch Signs

title: Board of Trustees
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Village of Weston Board of Trustees was scheduled to meet on May 18, 2026, to consider significant changes to park and recreation impact fees following a public hearing, review numerous license renewals for the 2026-2027 term, and address multiple infrastructure projects including construction proposals for intersection and road reconstruction projects.

Approval of April 21, 2026, Board of Trustees Meeting Minutes

discussions.item: Park and Recreation Impact Fees Public Hearing
discussions.body: The Board was scheduled to hold a public hearing on proposed significant increases to park and recreation impact fees for new residential development. The proposed changes would raise single-family fees from $300 to $761, duplex fees from $250 to $563 per unit, and multi-family fees from $250 to $495 per unit, based on the Village's 2022 Public Facilities Needs Assessment.

The Board was expected to consider a resolution expressing support for a long-term, sustainable funding solution to Wisconsin's Transportation Fund. This resolution was set to address state-level transportation infrastructure funding concerns.

discussions.item: Site Plan Review for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department & Municipal Court Facility
discussions.body: The Board was scheduled to review and potentially act on the site plan for a new Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court Facility at 3600 Community Center Drive. This represents a significant public safety infrastructure project for the community.

discussions.item: Farmers Market Location at Kennedy Park
discussions.body: The Board was set to discuss feedback on the Farmers Market location at Kennedy Park and recommended follow-up actions. This item was designated for discussion only with no action expected.

discussions.item: Raze Order Request for 4104 Shorey Avenue
discussions.body: The Board was expected to consider a raze order request for the property at 4104 Shorey Avenue. Raze orders typically require demolition of structures deemed unsafe or in violation of building codes.

The Board was scheduled to consider Ordinance No. 26-008 amending Chapter 18 Business regulations regarding Mobile Food Vendors License requirements. This was set to update licensing procedures for food truck and mobile vendor operations.

The Board was expected to review and act on construction services proposals for two major infrastructure projects: the Alderson and Jelinek Intersection Project and the Bloedel Ave Reconstruction Project.

discussions.item: Intergovernmental Agreement for Military Road Construction
discussions.body: The Board was scheduled to consider an intergovernmental agreement related to Military Road construction, which typically involves coordination with neighboring municipalities or county government on shared roadway projects.

The Board was set to discuss potential interest in completing CliftonStrengths assessments and training, a professional development tool designed to help identify individual strengths and improve team dynamics.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda, allowing persons to address the Board for up to three minutes regarding non-agenda items, with time extensions permitted at the Chief Presiding Officer's discretion. Additional public comment opportunity was scheduled during the Park and Recreation Impact Fees public hearing.
actionItems: Scheduled to vote on Ordinance No. 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015 amending park and recreation impact fees following public hearing
Expected to consider approval of multiple license renewals for 2026-2027 licensing term including alcohol, tobacco, lodging, and adult establishment licenses
Scheduled to vote on Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation fund solutions
Expected to consider site plan approval for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court Facility
Scheduled to vote on construction services proposals for Alderson/Jelinek intersection and Bloedel Ave reconstruction projects
Expected to consider intergovernmental agreement for Military Road construction
Scheduled to vote on raze order request for 4104 Shorey Avenue
Expected to consider ordinance amendments regarding mobile food vendors, nuisances, bicycles, and standing committees

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Call to Order
Approve the Minutes
Audit of the Bills
Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Public and Student Comment
Employee Handbook Updates for 2026-27 (Action Requested)
Recommendation for Preliminary 2026-27 Budget (Action Requested)
2026-2027 Paid Meal Price Update (Action Requested)
Activities & Athletics Code (Action Requested)
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)
Riverview RFP Approval (Action Requested)
Adjourn

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on Employee Handbook Updates for 2026-27
Board was expected to vote on the Preliminary 2026-27 Budget recommendation
Board was expected to vote on 2026-2027 Paid Meal Price Update
Board was expected to vote on Activities & Athletics Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling revisions
Board was expected to vote on Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Riverview RFP Approval for HVAC and roofing project

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Call to Order
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Preliminary Discussion Regarding Potential Litigation 19.85 (g)
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate
Adjourn

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Call to Order
Roll Call
Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Reading of the Mission Statement
Excellence in Action: Maine Elementary
Public and Student Comment
Approve Consent Agenda (Action Requested)
Old/Recurring Business
New Business
Open Forum
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Adjourn

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan
Board was expected to vote on 2026-27 Teacher Contract Approvals
Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff
Board was expected to vote on WAMCS Charter School Contract Renewal for 2026-2031
Board was expected to potentially take action following closed session regarding non-renewal notices and contract evaluations

title: Water Works Commission Meeting
overview: The Wausau Water Works Commission approved the 2027-2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility, along with two sole source agreements for cross connection control services and polymer chemicals. The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

agenda.item: Consideration of the minutes of the preceding meeting(s) - April 8, 2026 Regular Wausau Water Works Commission Minutes
Director's Reports - Update on Lead Service Line Replacements
Director's Reports - Wastewater Project Updates: Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station and Lift Station Assessment w/ Clark Dietz

Discussion regarding the Wastewater - Final WPDES Permit Issued with a permit term of five years

discussions.body: The commission approved the minutes from the April 8, 2026 regular meeting. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Update on Lead Service Line Replacements
discussions.body: The director provided an update on lead service line replacements. This was an informational report with no action required.

The director reported on wastewater projects including the Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station, and Lift Station Assessment with Clark Dietz. This was an informational report with no action required.
The commission approved the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Renewal Agreement for HydroCorp Cross Connection Control Program
discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source renewal agreement for HydroCorp Non-Residential Cross Connection Control Program. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA Polymers
discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA polymers used to thicken solids in the Belt Filter Presses and Gravity Belt Thickeners. The motion passed.
The commission discussed the final WPDES permit issued with a five-year term. This was a discussion item with no formal action taken.

actionItems: Implement 2027-2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility
Execute sole source renewal agreement with HydroCorp for Non-Residential Cross Connection Control Program

Next meeting scheduled for June 2, 2026 at 11:00 AM

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Arbor Day Proclamation
Week of the Young Child Proclamation
National Preservation Month Proclamation
Workers Memorial Day Proclamation
March 24, 2026 Regular Common Council Minutes
April 14, 2026 Regular Common Council Minutes
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.
